utterance: When the pipe gets narrower the speed has to go up — but I can ignore density because the fluid doesn't change.
label: no_density

utterance: Since A1 v1 = A2 v2 holds, density doesn't really matter for the flow rate calculation.
label: no_density

utterance: If the pressure drops then automatically the velocity should drop too — they always move together.
label: pressure_velocity_same_direction

utterance: Lower pressure means lower speed in pipes, that's just how it works.
label: pressure_velocity_same_direction

utterance: Conservation of mass means the flow rate is always perfectly the same in every pipe regardless of geometry.
label: mass_volume_flow_conflation

utterance: The volume per second has to be conserved no matter what so volumetric and mass flow are interchangeable here.
label: mass_volume_flow_conflation

utterance: I think Bernoulli works fine even when the fluid is highly compressible because pressure is what matters.
label: bernoulli_for_compressible

utterance: Compressibility doesn't affect Bernoulli's equation in any meaningful way for our problem.
label: bernoulli_for_compressible

utterance: There's no friction at all in real pipes — you can just ignore viscosity completely.
label: viscosity_negligible

utterance: Viscosity terms can always be dropped because they're tiny.
label: viscosity_negligible

utterance: Continuity says rho times A1 v1 equals rho times A2 v2; with constant density the rhos cancel out.
Bernoulli connects pressure, velocity, and elevation along a streamline for incompressible inviscid flow.
If A1 is twice A2 and we know v1, then v2 is 2 times v1 by mass conservation.
I'd start by writing the continuity equation between sections 1 and 2.
Pressure is force per unit area; in the pipe it pushes the fluid along.
Density rho is mass per unit volume — for water at room temperature it's about 1000 kg per cubic meter.
Once I have v2 from continuity I can plug it into Bernoulli to find P2.
We are assuming horizontal flow so the rho g h terms drop out from both sides.
I want to use the continuity equation A1 times v1 equals A2 times v2 because area and velocity are inversely related.
Bernoulli's equation gives me P1 plus half rho v1 squared equals P2 plus half rho v2 squared along a streamline.
